voices.text: Owners consistently praise the soft ride and quiet cabin — it soaks up bad city roads better than most rivals.
NA petrol AT owners report 9–11 km/l in city traffic, noticeably below the brochure figure; highway runs do better.
Hyundai's service network gets high marks — predictable costs and easy availability even in smaller towns.
Popular variants routinely carry 1–3 month waiting periods, and dealers push accessories to jump the queue.
Owners love the styling and the loaded top trims — the panoramic sunroof and 360 camera get mentioned a lot.
The firm suspension is the most common complaint — sharp bumps and potholes thud into the cabin more than in a Creta.
1.5 diesel owners report effortless highway cruising and 17–19 km/l on long runs.
Scattered reports of infotainment freezes and rattles; service response varies by dealer.
Strong-hybrid owners regularly see 20+ km/l in city driving — the single biggest reason they recommend it.
The hybrid's smaller boot (255 L) frustrates road-trippers; rear seat width is also tighter than rivals.
Smooth in town, but owners say the NA petrol feels strained on quick overtakes with a full load.
Maruti's low maintenance costs and dense service network remain a recurring plus point.
Like its Maruti twin, the strong hybrid's 20+ km/l city economy is what owners talk about most.
Owners cite Toyota's reliability reputation and longer standard warranty as the reason they paid the premium over the Grand Vitara.
Comfortable for four; the middle rear passenger and boot space draw complaints on the hybrid.
No ventilated seats on some trims and a basic infotainment compared to Korean rivals.
Owners praise the light controls, good visibility and absorbent ride — easy to live with daily.
The 1.5 NA petrol is smooth but unexciting; CVT owners note a rubber-band feel when pushed.
No sunroof options below top trim, no 360 camera, and cabin plastics feel built to a price.
Low niggle count and fuss-free service keep long-term owners loyal.
The 1.5 TSI DSG is the enthusiast pick of the segment — owners rave about the punch and body control.
A vocal minority report DSG overheating or jerky low-speed behaviour, and out-of-warranty repair costs worry owners.
Doors-shut-like-a-vault solidity and stable high-speed manners come up in nearly every review.
Tight rear seat for three and a sparse feature list versus Korean rivals at the same money.
Owners highlight the planted handling and 5-star BNCAP rating — it feels engineered, not assembled.
Skoda service has improved with capped-price plans, but parts still cost more than Maruti/Hyundai equivalents.
The 1.0 TSI returns a genuine 14–16 km/l for most owners while still feeling eager.
Three abreast in the back is a squeeze, and rear AC performance in peak summer draws complaints.
The coupe styling turns heads — owners say it photographs like a car a segment above.
Touchscreen freezes, sensor warnings and minor electrical gremlins are the most common early-ownership complaints.
Having the only diesel in the coupe-SUV space wins over high-mileage buyers; 16–18 km/l reported.
The sloping roof costs taller rear passengers some headroom — test-sit before booking.
Owners can't believe the feature list for the price — sunroof, level-2 ADAS and 360 camera undercut everyone.
The suspension takes broken roads better than any rival in the class, a consistent owner highlight.
Panel gaps and interior plastics lag the polish of Korean rivals; some rattles reported early.
Mahindra dealer experience is inconsistent — great in metros, patchy in smaller towns.
The red leatherette interior and big screen feel premium — owners say it punches above its price inside.
Weak resale values and uncertainty around the brand keep coming up in long-term owner posts.
The 1.3 turbo is quick but thirsty — 8–10 km/l city figures reported by AT owners.
ADAS and the AI assistant at this price point still undercut most rivals.
The 5-star crash rating is the single most cited reason owners chose the Nexon for their families.
Touchscreen lag, reverse camera dropouts and sensor errors remain the top complaints post-facelift.
Diesel owners love the mid-range punch and report 17–19 km/l on highway runs.
The AMT is fine in relaxed driving but head-nods in traffic; the DCA is smoother but pricier.
Cheap service, anywhere-in-India network and strong resale make it the default safe choice for owners.
The 1.5 NA petrol is smooth but feels flat with AC on and a full load, say highway drivers.
CNG owners report running costs under ₹2.5/km — unbeatable for high-mileage commuters.
Hard plastics everywhere and missing premium features at the top end disappoint some upgraders.
Compact size, light steering and good visibility make it a favourite for first-time and city drivers.
The tight rear bench is the most repeated complaint — fine for kids, cramped for three adults.
Quick and smooth when cold, but some owners report hesitation in crawling traffic.
Hyundai service consistency keeps overall ownership stress low.
Ventilated seats, Bose audio and sunroof in a sub-4m — owners feel they got a segment-above cabin.
Like the Seltos, the firm low-speed ride over potholes is the most common gripe.
The only diesel automatic in the class — owners doing long commutes swear by it.
Better boot than rivals but rear knee-room is tight for taller families.
Owners love the high seating, 5-star rating and the way it shrugs off broken roads at this price.
The 1.2 NA three-cylinder runs out of breath above 100 km/h — owners call it a city car at heart.
The under-floor twin CNG tanks keep the boot usable — a unique practical win owners highlight.
Three-cylinder thrum is always present; acceptable to most, annoying to some.
Owners consistently call it the most car for the least money in the segment.
Renault's shrinking dealer and service footprint is the biggest long-term worry owners voice.
The 1.0 turbo CVT is peppy once rolling but drones under hard acceleration.
405 L is class-best — luggage space wins family approval.
The entry price keeps stunning buyers — owners say nothing else offers SUV looks this cheap.
Hard, hollow-feeling plastics and inconsistent panel fit are the trade-off owners accept for the price.
The 1.0 turbo feels eager, though refinement trails the Koreans; NA version best avoided per owners.
Like Renault, Nissan's thin service network makes out-of-metro owners nervous.
The City's rear bench remains the family benchmark — owners call it the most comfortable seat under ₹20L.
The free-revving 1.5 petrol and light controls earn near-universal owner praise; 14–16 km/l reported.
Speed breakers need respect with five aboard — scraping reports are common on tall breakers.
Owners feel ADAS and sunroof arrive only at steep top-trim prices versus rivals.
The 160 PS turbo makes it the quickest sedan under ₹20L — enthusiasts among owners love it.
The futuristic styling splits opinion sharply — owners either love or apologise for it.
Ventilated seats, dual screens and ADAS — owners say nothing in the segment matches the kit.
Elderly family members find the low seating hard to get in and out of, a recurring family complaint.
1.5 TSI GT owners describe it as a junior GTI — the segment's driver's car.
High-speed stability and crash-test confidence are owner highlights.
The familiar DSG reliability anxiety follows the GT; extended warranty is considered essential by owners.
Parts and service cost more than Honda/Hyundai, though intervals are reasonable.
Owners praise the ride-handling balance — compliant over bad roads yet composed at speed.
The 150 PS TSI with DSG is repeatedly called the best engine in any sub-₹20L sedan.
Good legroom but rear AC vents struggle in May–June heat, say owners in hot cities.
Higher parts pricing versus Japanese rivals remains the main ownership gripe.
Nothing commands road respect like it — owners say traffic literally makes way.
The 2.2 diesel's effortless cruising and 14–16 km/l highway economy win unanimous praise.
Jump seats are punishment for adults and boot space with all rows up is nearly nil.
Rattles, software bugs and inconsistent dealer service temper the love affair.
Owners call the 200 PS petrol and 185 PS diesel unmatched performance-per-rupee in India.
Level-2 ADAS works well on highways but false braking events on Indian roads spook some owners.
Long waits and delivery-time price changes frustrated many early bookers.
Genuine 7-seater comfort and a composed highway ride make it the family favourite.
Owners say it still looks the most expensive car in its price band, especially post-facelift.
The Kryotec diesel is strong and efficient, but city buyers wish for the petrol that never came.
Screen blackouts, sensor faults and software bugs are the recurring dark side of ownership.
The OMEGA-arc platform's flat, planted ride draws favourable comparisons with Europeans.
The huge panoramic sunroof and sofa-like seats make owners describe it as a lounge on wheels.
The 14-inch screen wows, though laggy responses and voice-assistant misses frustrate daily users.
Soft suspension means pronounced lean in corners — drivers who hustle dislike it.
Owners repeatedly cite how much metal and kit the money buys versus XUV700 and Harrier.
Owners are thrilled with the lounge-like rear seats and high headroom; it feels like an upscale sofa.
The massive 15.6-inch display is loved by techies, but older drivers find doing basics (like adjusting AC) on screens annoying.
The 38kWh pack yields a reliable 230–250 km real range in city stop-and-go commuting, which fits most needs.
Very plush over bumps at low speed, but it feels floaty and rolls quite a bit on high-speed expressway curves.
Owners love the towering driving position and aggressive design; other road users give way instantly.
Unlike the 3-door Thar, the 5-door Roxx is praised by spouses for its rear legroom and decent 447L boot.
It is built on a body-on-frame Scorpio-N chassis; low-speed ride over side-to-side bumps can toss passengers around.
The diesel delivers an acceptable 11–13 km/l in city traffic, but the petrol version gets very thirsty (7–9 km/l).
Owners consistently report 18–21 km/l in the city and 24+ km/l on highways — highly economical.
Frugal engine, but some owners complain about mild cabin vibrations at idle compared to the previous 4-cylinder.
6 airbags and ESC standard across the range is highly appreciated for a budget hatchback.
The historic 5-star crash safety score is the primary reason many safety-conscious buyers picked it.
The segment-first single-pane electric sunroof is a massive hit with kids and younger buyers.
It shares the Swift's Z12E 3-cylinder engine; it is efficient but sounds vocal when revved hard.
The automatic version is widely praised as the smoothest and most stress-free commuter sedan under ₹10 Lakh.
Standard 6 airbags and 5-star BNCAP capability give owners huge peace of mind.
Excellent in town, but the NA i-VTEC lacks mid-range punch; it needs to be revved high to overtake on highways.
Feels typically solid like larger European SUVs, door shut is reassuring.
Surprisingly large boot for a sub-4m car, fits multiple bags easily.
Enthusiastic power delivery but idle vibrations reveal it's a 3-cylinder.
Extremely economical to drive, charging costs are negligible compared to petrol.
The panoramic sunroof makes it feel airy and premium inside.
Smooth on flat roads, but gets jittery over sharp potholes.
The sloping roofline gives it a sporty coupe look that stands out.
Easily delivers over 16 km/l in heavy city traffic, very frugal.
Plastics on the lower dashboard feel hard and scratchy.
Perfect for long-distance cruising, rear seats are incredibly comfortable.
Done over 1.5 Lakh km and it still runs like new. Zero breakdowns.
Clutch is a bit heavy, miss having an automatic option in traffic.
Excellent headroom, easy ingress and egress for elderly parents.
Engine is super quiet at city speeds, very smooth.
Fine for relaxed cruising, but lacks quick highway overtaking power.
Turns heads everywhere. Design looks like a concept car.
Flat floor and sliding center console make it feel like a spacious lounge.
Need to be careful over tall speedbreakers when fully loaded.
The big central screen is responsive, and software updates are frequent.
Very light and easy in the city, but feels artificial on the highway.
Crazy acceleration. The dual-motor version pulls like a sports car.
Great EV product, but VinFast dealerships are still sparse.
2.0 TFSI has excellent grunt, gearshifts are lightning-fast.
quattro AWD offers incredible confidence and grip on wet roads.
Boot is decent, but rear legroom is only average for this price.
Super silent cabin, almost zero road or wind noise makes it very relaxing.
Suspension setup is stellar, glides over broken patches smoothly.
Heavy SUV + petrol engine means fuel bills are quite high.
